DÉLÉGATIOM DU BRÉSIL ar à LA CONFERENCE DE LA PAIX . / Monsieur le PASA

À l'occasion de la signsture du Traité de Paix, J'ai eu l'hon-

ie { ille£ 1919

neur de faire savoir au Secrétariat Général que l'un des Plénipotentinireg du Brésil , Mensieur Raul Femamies avait été forcé de s'abgenter pour un motif de néoésasitó absolue,sa femme étant tombée gravement malade en Suisse.

J'ai fait demander gi Mr.¥ernanles ne pouvait signer aprés la céremdnie du 28 Juin,vu que son &conu était apposé au traîté et que, | du point de vue de l'engagement i1nternatiorel,la signature du Brésil y figurait déjà par celles du Président de la Délégation et de l'un des Déléguég,Mr.Rodrigo Octavio.La&a réponse qui m'a été cormuniquée mentionnait que,vu les oirconstanceg,l'appel daaplénipotentiaires se ferait par délégation et non nominalement,et que 18 signature de

Mr.Fernanies pourrait Stre dispensée pour quelque temps.

Mr.Fernandes vient de rentrer à Faris,se basant aur ces assurancea et est pr8t à signer le Traité.L'on vient de me faire savoir toutefois que c'est trôp tard et que les copies certifiées conforme ont été déjà été distribuées sux puissances signataires sans que

Xe nom de Ér.Fernsndes y figure Binon dang l'énumeration des plénipotentisires.

DELEGATION DU BRÉSIL

À LA CONFERENCE DE LA PAIX

» COPIE, Paris, le 7 juillet 1919.

PERSONNELLE,

Monsieur le Président,

La veille de la signature du Traité de Paix,

l'un des Flénipotentiaires du Brésil, Mr, R.FERNANDES, dont la femme est trés gravement malade en Sulsse, a reçu des télégrammes lui disant qu'une crise était survenue et qu'elle se trouvait á la derniére extremité, À ce moment, le sceau de Mr, R.FERNANDES avait déja été apposé au Traité, Sur l'assurance qu'il pourrait signer À son retour, il est parti pour Lausanne dans l'état d’es- prit que vous pouvez vous figurer, Le danger immédiat étant écarté, 11 s'est empressé de rentrer À Paris, où il est arrivé ce matin, pour donner $a signature, Ja viens de savolr que le Secrétaire .Général souléve des difficultés 4 celá, en disant, ce qui est pare faltement exact, que les copies dûment certifiés ont déjà été dise tribuéB aux Puissances signataires, Je crois qu'il y aurait moyen de compléter les signatures de la Délézation Brésilienne si le See crétolre Général se chargeait de faire aux Puissanceg intéressées la communication du cas de force majeure, et en déciarant que la signature complémentaire a été donnée le .., courant,

C'est co que j'ai exposé dans la lettre a Mr, Dutasta, lettre dont je prends la liberté de vous envoyer la copie ci-Jjointe,

Je me permets donc, Monsieur le Président, de Tous

prier de bien vouloir examiner le cas avec blenveillance, car la Dé.

légation que Je pbéside aurait à coeur de voir figurer sur le Trale té les signatures, de tous ces Membres ,

Veuillez &gréer, Monsieur le Président, etc,

{signé} CALOGERAS,

A Bon Excellence Monsieur GEORGES CLEMENCEAU,

Président de la Conférence de la Paix, eto, etc,

ARMISTIC DAY EXHIBITION AT THE LIBRARY OF CONGRESS IO INCLUDE

ORIGINA DOCUMENTS OF THE TREATIES OF MUNSTER AND TEESATLLES

A bracial exhibition marking the twenty-sixth anniversary of the

Armistice which ended the firat World War will open at the Library of Con grese on Saturday, November 11, Archibald Macleish, the Librarian of Con gress, arnounced todey, The exhibition, arranged through the courtesy of the irenst Minister, M. Henry Hoppenot, will include the German ratift.

cation of the Treaty of Versailles, the proces-verbal under which thre

Treaty of Tersailles was deposited in Paris and the engrosaed, signed and sealed original of the Troaty of Minster, one of the threo treaties which establiahed the Peaco of Westphalia in 1649,

These documonta from the

bronch National Archives, have been lent to

the library of Congress by the

Franch Minister in Washington, None has ovor before been exhibited in the

United States.

As part of the Peaco of Westphalia, the Ireaty of Münster brought an end to tho Thirty Joara' War (1618-1648), the last of the Wars of Religion and tho first of the great and goneral European Wars which have since expandod to bocome World Wars. Beginning as a struggle between Protostants and Catholics for the control of Bohemia, it gradually diew in mast of the great powers of the era, and was in its lator stages primarily a contost for territory botween France and Swaden on the ono hand, and dustria and

Most of the fighting took place in Gormany, in that

day the passive victim of other nations' Importalism, and the desolation

The Peace of Wostphalia, which finally

stoppod a conflict of which all parties were weary, 1s a landmark in the development of international law and the establiahment of diplomatic usage,

Ihe pernanent representation of nations by diplomatic envoys at each otherst capitals dates from 1648,

Becauso of the variety and complexity of the intoresta involved, the lack of seiviceable precedents, making the peace was a lorg and difficult business.

The difforencos botweon France and Spain could not be ra

golvod and they remained at war until 1659, Austria inaistod on conducting separate nogotiations with her two allied enemies, Irance and Sweden, but was finally inducod, in 1641, to hold tbese at the two testphalian towns of ünstar and Osnabrück, less

chen talrty milos apart, so that the Fronch en

ove (at lunster) and the Swedish envors (at Osnabruck could easily commu nicate with each other. Spain and Holland participated, and were the first to concludo thoir treaty of poace m at Minster on January 30, 1648,

Ihe Peace Congross had boon summoned for July 1643, but all the delegates did not arrive until April 1644, and the first year was largel, occupied with questions of precedonce and proceduro.

Since the negotia

tions wore conductod very formally and on paper, and Important questions were often referred home, three and a half more years were required to work out the torme of the treaties.

The mediators at Minster were a

repregentative of the Pope and one of the Republie of Venice, The Treaty of Muneter transferred all Imperial (the ruler of Austria was also the

Emperor of

the Holy Roman Impire of the German Nation) rights in Alsace

to Francs, beginning a process of annexation which was finally completed in 1697.

The Ireaty of Osnabrück, signed the same day de Munster, gave

several German territories to Sweden,

The Treaty of Vergailles, which fized responalbility for the firet

World War upon Gerneny, transferred several portions of her territory to other povers, Iigidly limited her armaments, and imposed heavy reparation paynents, was a bitter pill for the German government to awallow,

German ministry fell, and the Allies bad to threaten a renowal of the war, before the German National Assembly would agree to the signature of the

Treaty, and even then, on June 23, 1919, 138 votes opposad the majority

Iven after the two German roprosontatives had signed the Treaty on Juno 28, 115 votes in the Asaonbly opposed the fatification Bill on

July 9.

The German Instrument of Ratlfication, datod July 9, 1919, will bo shown at the Library as well as the procds-vorbal of January 10, 1920, under which the various ratifications woro deposited.

Phe cornan Hatift-

cation is signed by Friedrich Ibert, Prosidont, and Gustav Bauer, Chancellot, of the German Republio.

A modern Instrument of Ratification is

eupposed to incorporate a copy of the Treaty to which it refors, and gince this one had to incorporato the 440-article Troaty of Versailles, a rather bulky document rosulted, Unlike the documents pertaining to the Treaty of Munster, which are manuscripts, those relating to the Treaty of Versailles are printed, sithough they bear manuscript signatures.

The Treaty of Vergaillos, betwaon Gormany and the Allied Powers, was signed in tho Hall of Mirrors on June 28, 1919.
